content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 7, 1995

Enrique R. Sanchez
Deputy County Counsel
County of Santa Barbara
105 East Anapamu Street, Suite 201
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-95-013

Dear Mr. Sanchez:

You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1/ Since your
letter is a general inquiry, we consider it to be a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

QUESTION

May the County of Santa Barbara adopt a single conflict of
interest code for all local agencies that are under the control of
and administered by the Santa Barbara County Board of Supervisors?

CONCLUSION

The County of Santa Barbara may adopt a single comprehensive
conflict of interest code for all county agencies. The conflict
of interest code must be approved by the Santa Barbara County
Board of Supervisors before it is effective.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Mr. Sanchez
Our File No. I-95-013
Page Two


FACTS

     Currently, each separate agency administered by the Santa
Barbara County Board of Supervisors (i.e., public works,
treasurer, special districts for which the board of supervisors
sits as the board of directors, etc.) has adopted an individual
conflict of interest code. The county is now proposing adoption
of a single code encompassing all county agencies. Each agency
would provide input into the code adoption process by developing
and updating exhibits to be added to the county's code. These
exhibits would include designated positions and disclosure
categories that pertain to each agency.

DISCUSSION

     Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code. A conflict of interest
code is not effective until it has been approved by the code
reviewing body. (Section 87303). The code reviewing body for
every county agency is the board of supervisors. (Section
82011(b)).

     Section 87301 states conflict of interest codes shall be
formulated at the most decentralized level possible, but without
precluding intra-departmental review. This section also gives
code reviewing bodies the authority to resolve questions regarding
the level of a department which should be deemed an "agency" for
purposes of Section 87300. Therefore, the board of supervisors,
acting in its capacity as the code reviewing body, may determine
that all departments and agencies within county government are a
single "agency" for purposes of Section 87300 and may approve a
single conflict of interest code covering all county agencies.

     Please note that the Commission has previously advised that
any agency (whose jurisdiction is solely within one county) which
is not a county agency and is structured as a separate local body
should create its own code. (Stamper Advice Letter, No. I-94-105,
copy enclosed). Section 82011(b) would still require the code for
a separate local body within one county to be approved by the
board of supervisors.

Mr. Sanchez
Our File No. I-95-013
Page Three


You have also requested that we review and comment on the
draft resolution you have prepared that proposes the concept of a
single comprehensive code.  The basic components of your proposed
resolution are outlined below.

     1)   Each county agency will be involved in preparing
exhibits to include the designated positions and
corresponding disclosure categories for those positions
for inclusion in the county's conflict of interest code.
Each county agency will designate an officer for
carrying out these duties and will inform the clerk-
recorder of this designation.

     2)   The clerk-recorder will be authorized to adopt, amend
and revise the code and submit the code to the board of
supervisors for approval.  After the code is approved by
the board of supervisors, the code will be maintained
for public inspection with the clerk-recorder.

     3)   The clerk-recorder or designee shall inform and provide
agency officers any notices of action required by the
Act, including biennial notices required by Section
87306.5.  All statements of economic interests will be
filed with an officer to be designated by the clerk-
recorder.

     4)   Each agency will retain a copy of the model standard
code (Regulation 18730) with the exhibits (list of
designated positions and disclosure categories) that
pertain to the agency.

     Section 87311 specifies that the review and preparation of
conflict of interest codes by local government agencies shall be
carried out under procedures which guarantee to officers,
employees, members and consultants of the agency and to residents
of the jurisdiction adequate notice and a fair opportunity to
present their views.  Pursuant to Section 87309, no conflict of
interest code or amendment can be approved by a code reviewing
body if it:

     (a)  Fails to provide reasonable assurance that all
foreseeable potential conflict of interest situations will be
disclosed or prevented;
     (b)  Fails to provide to each affected person a clear
and specific statement of his duties under the Code; or
     (c)  Fails to adequately differentiate between
designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.

Mr. Sanchez
Our File No. I-95-013
Page Four


     The Act does not delineate any other specific procedures that
local code reviewing bodies must undertake before approving codes.
As long as local code reviewing bodies comply with the procedures
set forth in Sections 87311 and 87309, they have discretionary
authority to implement their own code adoption procedures. The
adoption procedures you have outlined do not appear to conflict
with any code adoption provisions under the Act.

     If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Dixie Howard
                                        Dixie Howard
                                        Political Reform Consultant
                                        Technical Assistance Division

Enclosure
